rationale: This example shows the reaction of a simple ketone (acetone) with an alpha-silyl carbanion, generated in situ from (chloromethyl)trimethylsilane and a strong base (represented here by elemental lithium, a common method for generating such organometallics). The resulting beta-hydroxysilane intermediate eliminates to form the corresponding alkene (isobutylene). This represents the core transformation of the Peterson olefination: converting a C=O bond into a C=C bond using a silicon-based reagent, and is a simple, acyclic, textbook case.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic aliphatic substrates (acetone and a TMS-methyllithium precursor) to avoid overlap with other named reactions. It avoids arenes (common in Wittig, Horner-Wadsworth-Emmons), complex ring systems, or other reactive functional groups. The explicit use of a trimethylsilyl group is a key feature that distinguishes it from phosphorus-based olefinations (Wittig, HWE) or sulfur-based ones (Julia-Kocienski).
